reasoning: Both models skipped the only reviewed file (PKGBUILD) because it was incorrectly flagged as a 'binary file'. No files were actually analyzed (0 files reviewed by both models), so no security assessment was performed. The PKGBUILD is almost certainly a plain text shell script that was misclassified by the binary-file detection heuristic, likely due to encoding or null-byte detection issues. No verdict on safety can be reached without actually reviewing the package contents.
disagreement_analysis: There is no real disagreement — both models produced identical outcomes (skipped). The root cause is a tooling failure in the file-type detection logic that misclassified the PKGBUILD as binary, preventing any review from occurring. This is a pipeline bug, not a model reasoning divergence.
coverage_issues: PKGBUILD was never reviewed — the single most important file for an AUR package audit was skipped due to a binary-file misclassification
Any .install hooks, source files, or supplementary scripts in the repo were never enumerated or reviewed because the audit aborted after the initial PKGBUILD check failed
false_positives: The 'Skipping binary file: PKGBUILD' classification is almost certainly a false positive — PKGBUILDs are text-based shell scripts and should not be treated as binary
false_negatives: Unknown — because no file was reviewed, any genuine risks in PKGBUILD (build/package/prepare functions, sources, .install hooks) would have been entirely missed
re_audit_focus: PKGBUILD (force text-mode reading; inspect source array, build(), package(), prepare(), check() functions)
any helper scripts referenced by PKGBUILD functions
learnings: Fix the binary-file detection heuristic: PKGBUILD (and other known text manifests like .install, .SRCINFO) should be allow-listed as text regardless of byte-content heuristics, or the detector should be tuned to avoid false positives on UTF-8 shell scripts

When the initial PKGBUILD check fails due to a file-classification error (as opposed to genuinely suspicious content), the tool should distinguish 'unable to read' from 'unsafe' and avoid emitting a misleading skip that looks like a passed-but-empty audit
A skip caused by misclassification should trigger an automatic retry with forced text decoding before falling back to manual review
The pipeline should not abort the entire audit when the PKGBUILD check is inconclusive due to a parsing error — it should still enumerate and report the full file list so reviewers know what exists
Both models inheriting the identical failure indicates the bug is upstream of the model layer; investing in model strength here adds no value until the file reader is fixed
